color_profiles.name: red-orange-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Fe2O3 (hematite) in the replacing chalcedony; oxidizing conditions during silicification.

color_profiles.name: yellow-brown-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: FeO(OH) goethite/limonite; partially hydrated iron oxides.

color_profiles.name: black-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: MnO2 (pyrolusite) or carbonaceous material preserved in low-oxygen conditions.

color_profiles.name: gray-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Low trace element content in pure silica replacement; minimal chromophores.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Cu or Cr in the replacing silica.

color_profiles.name: agatized-wood
color_profiles.cause: Multiple silica precipitation events with varying trace element concentrations, producing banding analogous to agate.

visual_notes: The preserved wood grain and annual rings are the defining visual feature — the stone tells a biological story. Cross-sections are among the most visually striking mineral-art objects, showing perfect tree anatomy in glowing chalcedony. Color zonation often follows the original wood structure.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some material fluoresces green or yellow under UV (chalcedony component)
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; some fibrous chalcedony replacements show weak chatoyancy
